summary: Jeff Atwood discusses implementing global exception handling for .NET web services, moving from Application_Error in global.asax to HttpModule for better abstraction and easier deployment. He explains how HttpModule allows adding exception handling to any ASP.NET website without recompiling—just by dropping a DLL and modifying web.config. He then tackles the harder problem of web service exception handling via SoapExtension, which modifies SOAP messages in-flight to communicate detailed error information back to clients. The solution works well for SOAP clients but not for browser-based testing.
key_points: HttpModule provides superior exception handling compared to Application_Error because it can be added to any ASP.NET site without recompilation
The .Source property of exceptions usually contains the assembly name that generated the error, which helps identify the source of problems
SoapExtension allows modifying SOAP messages in-flight to inject detailed exception information into the <detail> node
Web services require different exception handling than websites because there's no visible UI to display error pages
The SoapExtension approach only works for real SOAP clients, not browser-based testing
money_quotes: Catching unhandled exceptions via HttpModule is a far better solution than using Application_Error in global.asax.
You can implement global unhandled exception handling on any ASP.NET website without recompiling.
This is critical for a web service, because unlike a website, there's no visible UI to present a helpful error page – and thus no way to diagnose the error without rooting through server files.
Be warned; you'd expect it to work the same in the browser, but it doesn't.
key_insight: Better error handling architecture often comes from choosing the right hook point in the framework pipeline, where HttpModule and SoapExtension provide more flexible and less invasive integration than application-level events.
